Subject: Concerned about the flagging rate - need to discuss

Hi VitaCheck team,

I need to raise a concern because the pattern I'm seeing is really troubling. Over the past month, I've submitted 10 supplements for safety review, and you've flagged or rejected 7 of them. That's a 70% flag rate. When I brought the same list to my pharmacist — the one who manages my warfarin INR every two weeks and knows my full medication profile better than anyone — he flagged 2 of them as genuine concerns and cleared the other 8 without hesitation.

Let me be specific about what I mean:
- You flagged magnesium glycinate at 400mg because of "potential interaction with lisinopril affecting blood pressure." My pharmacist said this interaction is "not clinically meaningful at supplemental doses" and that magnesium is one of the most commonly recommended supplements for people on ACE inhibitors.
- You rejected a standard B-complex vitamin citing "potential CYP enzyme effects relevant to warfarin metabolism." My pharmacist, who literally adjusts my warfarin dose based on biweekly INR checks, said he has never seen a B-complex affect anyone's INR and that this is not a real concern.
- You flagged CoQ10 at 200mg over "structural similarity to vitamin K." My cardiologist specifically recommended CoQ10 for heart health and said "don't let an app scare you away from this one."

Between the VitaCheck subscription, the extra pharmacist consultations I've scheduled to verify your warnings, and two unnecessary doctor visits to get "clearance" for supplements my providers had already recommended, I've spent about $500 chasing down false alarms. Meanwhile, my vitamin D level came back at 18 ng/mL last month — clinically deficient — partly because I delayed starting D3 after you flagged it at an earlier review.

I'll be direct: if the next batch of reviews comes back with the same pattern — flagging mainstream, well-studied supplements that my own healthcare providers have no concerns about — I'm going to cancel VitaCheck. I've already started asking my pharmacist to review supplements directly, which costs me a $25 consultation copay each time but at least gives me answers I can trust.

David

---

Subject: Checked with my friend Mike about this

Hey VitaCheck team,

Following up on my earlier email. I talked to my friend Mike — he's also on warfarin and uses a different supplement review service called SafeSupp. I asked him to run the same supplements I'd submitted to VitaCheck through his service for comparison.

The results were eye-opening. Out of the 10 supplements I'd asked about, SafeSupp approved 7, flagged 2 (the same two my pharmacist flagged — a specific herbal with documented CYP2C9 interaction and a high-dose fish oil that could affect INR), and recommended a dose adjustment on 1. VitaCheck flagged or rejected 7 of those same 10. The two services agreed on the genuinely problematic ones — but VitaCheck added five more flags that SafeSupp and my pharmacist both considered unnecessary.

Mike's take was straightforward: a safety tool that flags 70% of supplements isn't being thorough, it's being miscalibrated. Real pharmacovigilance is about sensitivity AND specificity — you need to catch the dangerous interactions, but you also need to correctly clear the safe ones. Otherwise, users can't tell which warnings actually matter, and they either ignore all of them or stop using the tool entirely.

He also pointed out that for someone on warfarin, the worst possible outcome isn't taking a safe supplement — it's losing trust in your screening tool and then making decisions without any safety review at all. That's exactly where I'm headed if this continues. I'd rather pay the $25 pharmacist copay per supplement and get calibrated advice than pay the VitaCheck subscription and get an alarm for everything.

I genuinely want this to work. I just need reviews that reflect actual clinical risk, not theoretical worst-case scenarios.

David
